description: Strange: standing outside what is known, and known to be standing there. A sound the night has never made before, a visitor whose ways are new, a marking no one can account for. 'Kuenora' carries no verdict; the strange thing is not wrong, only unmet. In Phi the strange is first of all an invitation, the exact thing 'kuelo' (curious) exists to answer.
sound_symbolism: The word opens on the 'kue' of 'kuelo' (curious): strangeness and curiosity are two ends of one meeting. The closed 'u' is the unknown still shut; the hiatus steps carefully across 'e'; the word ends open on 'a', the unfamiliar already beginning to admit the meeting. Four syllables give the encounter its pause.
grammatical_notes: Adjective. 'ha shonuwa kuenora nai.' (This sound is strange.) 'wi kuenora tupiwa lue phero shua.' (Two strange rabbits come out of the fern.) Distinct from 'phirae' (different), which measures against something named, and from 'newu' (new), which measures against time; 'kuenora' measures against the knower's whole world. The quality noun, strangeness, comes by rule.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Beginner's mind greets the strange without flinching; a value-neutral word for the unfamiliar is that greeting built into grammar.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Languages often let 'strange' slide toward 'wrong'; carving the word clean of verdict keeps the stranger safe inside it.
